coredata.dc:title: Combination of poly (ε–caprolactone) grafted graphene quantum dots–based dispersive solid phase extraction followed by dispersive liquid–liquid microextraction for extraction of some pesticides from fruit juices prior to their quantification by gas chromatography
coredata.dc:creator: Mohebbi, Ali
Farajzadeh, Mir Ali
Mahmoudzadeh, Ahad
Etemady, Asma
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a poly (ε–caprolactone) grafted graphene quantum dots–based dispersive solid phase extraction coupled with dispersive liquid–liquid microextraction has been developed to extract nine pesticides including penconazole, chlorpyrifos, haloxyfop–R–methyl, oxadiazon, clodinafop–propargyl, diniconazole, fenazaquin, fenpropathrin, and fenoxaprop–P–ethyl from different packed fruit juice samples (pomegranate, watermelon, apricot, grape, sour cherry, apple, and orange juices). In the first step, the analytes are adsorbed onto the sorbent and after that the sorbent is settled down by centrifugation and eluted with an appropriate elution solvent (methanol). In the second step, the obtained eluant is mixed with an appropriate preconcentration solvent (1,1,2–TCE) at µL–level and the mixture is spread into deionized water. After centrifugation, a fraction of the sedimented phase is removed with a microsyringe and injected into the separation system for analysis. The properties of the sorbent were characterized using various techniques including X–ray diffraction, Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometry, scanning electron microscope, Brunauer–Emmett–Teller nitrogen sorption, and differential scanning calorimetry. Under the optimum conditions, the method indicated low limits of detection (0.32–0.76 ng mL–1) and quantification (1.1–2.6 ng mL–1), high enrichment factors (680–840 and 340–420 for deionized water and fruit juices, respectively), acceptable extraction recoveries (68–84%), and satisfactory relative standard deviations (≤ 8.4% for intra– and inter–day precisions at a concentration of 10 ng mL–1 of each analyte).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dispersive liquid–liquid microextraction
Dispersive solid phase extraction
Fruit juice
Gas chromatography
Pesticide

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of poly (ε–caprolactone) grafted graphene quantum dots
Selection of type and volume of elution/disperser solvent
Selection the type of preconcentration solvent
Optimization of centrifugation speed and time
Application for analysis of real samples
Comparison of the method with other approaches

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Research Council of University of Tabriz

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the Research Council of University of Tabriz for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the synthesized sorbent (a) FTIR spectrum, (b) XRD pattern, (c) DSC plot, and (d) SEM image.

Typical GC–FID chromatograms of: (A) standard solution (50 mg L−1 in 1,1,2–TCE, each pesticide), (B) grape juice spiked with 25 ng mL−1 of each pesticide, and (C) unspiked grape juice after performing the proposed method, except chromatogram A in which direct injection without preconcentration was used. Peaks identification: (1) chlorpyrifos, (2) penconazole, (3) haloxyfop–R–methyl, (4) oxadiazon, (5) diniconazole, (6) clodinafop–propargyl, (7) fenpropathrin, (8) fenazaquin, and (9) fenoxaprop–P–ethyl.
Typical GC–TIC–MS chromatogram of grape juice after performing the proposed method (A), scan 948 (retention time 11.286 min) (B), and mass spectrum of penconazole (C).
Quantitative features of the developed method for the selected pesticides.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The results obtained for intra– (n = 6) and inter–day (n = 4) precisions at a concentration of 10 ng mL–1 of each analyte.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of assays to check the sample matrix effects for the selected pesticides. Penconazole content of the grape juice was subtracted. All samples were diluted with deionized water at a ratio of 1:1 before being analyzed.

row.entry: All samples were spiked with each analyte at a concentration of 3 ng mL–1.
All samples were spiked with each analyte at a concentration of 10 ng mL–1.
All samples were spiked with each analyte at a concentration of 25 ng mL–1.
All samples were spiked with each analyte at a concentration of 50 ng mL–1.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the presented method with the other methods used in preconcentration and determination of the studied pesticides.

head.ce:title: Combination of poly (ε–caprolactone) grafted graphene quantum dots–based dispersive solid phase extraction followed by dispersive liquid–liquid microextraction for extraction of some pesticides from fruit juices prior to their quantification by gas chromatography

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cDepartment of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a poly (ε–caprolactone) grafted graphene quantum dots–based dispersive solid phase extraction coupled with dispersive liquid–liquid microextraction has been developed to extract nine pesticides including penconazole, chlorpyrifos, haloxyfop–R–methyl, oxadiazon, clodinafop–propargyl, diniconazole, fenazaquin, fenpropathrin, and fenoxaprop–P–ethyl from different packed fruit juice samples (pomegranate, watermelon, apricot, grape, sour cherry, apple, and orange juices). In the first step, the analytes are adsorbed onto the sorbent and after that the sorbent is settled down by centrifugation and eluted with an appropriate elution solvent (methanol). In the second step, the obtained eluant is mixed with an appropriate preconcentration solvent (1,1,2–TCE) at µL–level and the mixture is spread into deionized water. After centrifugation, a fraction of the sedimented phase is removed with a microsyringe and injected into the separation system for analysis. The properties of the sorbent were characterized using various techniques including X–ray diffraction, Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometry, scanning electron microscope, Brunauer–Emmett–Teller nitrogen sorption, and differential scanning calorimetry. Under the optimum conditions, the method indicated low limits of detection (0.32–0.76 ng mL–1) and quantification (1.1–2.6 ng mL–1), high enrichment factors (680–840 and 340–420 for deionized water and fruit juices, respectively), acceptable extraction recoveries (68–84%), and satisfactory relative standard deviations (≤ 8.4% for intra– and inter–day precisions at a concentration of 10 ng mL–1 of each analyte).

ce:section.ce:para: Pesticides are known as the substances which used to combat or kill any group of pests including fungicides, insects, herbicides or acaricides in order to improve the crops quality and yield [1]. But at the same time, the misuse of pesticides can result in unacceptable levels of them in the products such as fruits and their by–products, which can endanger the human health because of their potential toxicity [2]. Considering these facts, monitoring and determination of pesticide residues in widely used food stuffs like fruits is of great importance for the sake of human health. Mostly, chromatographic methods such as gas chromatography (GC) [3,4] and high–performance liquid chromatography [5,6] are used for the quantitative analysis of pesticides. Although these methods are sensitive but unfortunately, they cannot handle samples directly regarding to the complex matrix of samples. Therefore, an additional step termed as sample preparation is also needed to achieve the necessary levels of sensitivity, enrichment, and clean–up [7]. So, the development of time saving and highly efficient analytical methods has become a vital issue in analytical chemistry. Liquid–liquid extraction (LLE) and solid phase extraction (SPE) are traditionally used as sample preparation methods for the extraction of various groups of analytes. Compared to LLE, SPE provides more advantages including a lower consumption of toxic organic solvents and shorter processing time [8]. SPE is a surface dependent method in which the analytes are adsorbed on a solid sorbent immobilized on a supporting phase such as cartridge or fiber. Despite the above–mentioned merits of SPE, this method has also some difficulties such as obstruction of cartridges, and necessity of pretreatment of sorbents prior to perform the procedure [9]. To circumvent these drawbacks, a new mode of SPE termed as dispersive solid phase extraction (DSPE) has been introduced [10]. In this method, there is no need to pack the solid sorbent into a cartridge and it directly dispersed into the sample solution which enables the sorbent to interact with whole of the sample, and avoiding channeling or blocking of cartridge occurred in traditional SPE [11,12]. After adsorbing the analytes, the sorbent particles are settled down by centrifugation and after that eluted with an appropriate organic solvent. In SPE–based procedures, selection of a sorbent is important and can affect extraction efficiency of developed method. In recent years sorbents such as carbon nanotubes [13], octadecylsilane [11], graphene [14], metal–organic frameworks [15], molecularly imprinted polymers [16], etc. have been proposed for using in DSPE. In addition, some magnetic sorbents were also used which were separated from the aqueous solution by applying an external magnetic field without performing centrifugation procedure [17,18]. Nevertheless, still the quests for novel sorbents have not been ceased and this field is interesting for the analytical scientists. DSPE is rapid, efficient, cost–effective, and simple method. But, DSPE also has some shortcomings. The major deficiency is that low enrichment factors (EFs) are achievable by performing DSPE by consuming relatively high volume of an elution solvent and led to high detection limits. Mostly, a combination of DSPE with a microextraction method like dispersive liquid–liquid microextraction (DLLME) has been used to overcome this problem [19,20]. In this mode, the sorbent is eluted with an appropriate organic solvent which acts as a disperser in the following DLLME step.

In this work, for the first time, a combination of poly (ε–caprolactone) grafted graphene quantum dots–based DSPE and DLLME is used as an efficient sample pretreatment method for the extraction of some pesticides from fruit juices before their quantification by gas chromatography–flame ionization detection (GC–FID). It should be mentioned that while the quantum dots have been widely used as fluorescent probes in analytical chemistry [21,22], their use as sorbents is relatively rare. Thus, the present work is an attempt to evaluate the quantum dots (polymer functionalized graphene quantum dots) usefulness as a sorbent for pesticides in DSPE method. Unfortunately, DSPE has relatively low EFs in most cases. Therefore to reach high EFs and low limits of detection (LODs) it was combined with DLLME. This combination was solved the problem associated with DLLME in its application in analysis of samples having complex matrices.

Clodinafop–propargyl, haloxyfop–R–methyl, fenoxaprop–P–ethyl, fenazaquin, oxadiazon, chlorpyrifos, fenpropathrin, diniconazole, and penconazole were purchased from Dr. Ehrenstorfer (Augsburg, Germany). Methanol, hydrochloric acid (37%, w/w), acetonitrile (ACN), isopropanol, chloroform, n–hexane, toluene, sulfuric acid, tetrahydrofuran, sodium hydroxide, sodium chloride, sodium nitrate, potassium permanganate, and hydrogen peroxide (30%, w/w) (all analytical reagent) were obtained from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). 1,1,1–Trichloroethane (1,1,1–TCE), 1,2–dibromoethane (1,2–DBE), and 1,1,2–trichloroethane (1,1,2–TCE) were from Janssen (Beerse, Belgium). Graphite powder (< 20 μm), iron (III) chloride, and stannous 2–ethylhexanoate [Sn(Oct)2] were from Sigma–Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). ε–Caprolactone (CL) monomer was also purchased from Sigma–Aldrich and was dried over calcium hydride by stirring and distilled under reduced pressure prior to use. A stock solution of the analytes was prepared in methanol at a concentration of 1000 mg L–1 of each analyte. Fresh working standard solutions at low concentrations were daily prepared by diluting the stock solution with deionized water. In addition, another standard solution of the analytes was prepared in 1,1,2–TCE (at a concentration of 50 mg L–1 of each analyte) and used for direct injection into the separation system to calculate EFs and extraction recoveries (ERs).

Samples including pomegranate, watermelon, apricot, grape, sour cherry, apple, and orange commercial juices were purchased from local supermarkets (Tabriz, Iran). The juices were centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min. The supernatants were diluted with deionized water at a ratio of 1:1 and then subjected to the extraction procedure. All samples were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C before their analysis.

Determination of the analytes was carried out on a Shimadzu 2014 gas chromatograph (Kyoto, Japan) equipped with a split/splitless injector and an FID. Helium (99.999%, Gulf Cryo, United Arab Emirates) was used as the carrier gas at a linear velocity of 30 cm s−1 and make up gas (30 mL min–1). The injection port and FID temperatures were adjusted at 300 °C. A DB–1 capillary column (100% dimethyl siloxane, 30 m × 0.25 mm i.d., and a film thickness of 0.25 µm) (Supelco, Bellefonte, USA) was used for the separation of the analytes. The oven temperature programming used for the separation of analytes was as follows: 190 °C for 7 min and then increased at 18 °C min–1 to 300 °C, held for 6 min. Hydrogen was generated using OPGU–1500S (Shimadzu, Japan) and utilized at a flow rate of 30 mL min−1. Air was used for FID at a flow rate of 300 mL min−1. All injections were performed in a splitless mode. Gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC–MS) analysis was carried out on an Agilent 7890A–5975C (Agilent Technologies, CA, USA) equipped with a split/splitless injector. MS operational conditions were: transfer line temperature, 260 °C; mass range, m/z 55–400; ionic source temperature, 250 °C; acquisition rate, 20 Hz; electron ionization at 70 eV; and detector voltage, –1700 V. The separation was carried out on an HP–1 MS capillary column (30 m × 0.25 mm i.d., and a film thickness of 0.25 μm) (Agilent Technologies, CA, USA). Helium was used as the carrier gas at a flow rate of 1.0 mL min–1. Other conditions were the same as those used in GC–FID analysis. A 1–μL microsyringe (zero dead volume, Hamilton, Switzerland) was used for the injection of the samples into GC–FID and GC–MS. pH measurements were performed using a Metrohm model 654 (Herisau, Switzerland). An ultrasonic water bath (Branson 3510, Danbury, CT, USA), an Universal 320 R centrifuge (Hettich, Kirchlengern, Germany), and an L46 vortex (Labinco, Breda, The Netherlands) were used for desorb the analytes from the sorbent, accelerating phase separation, and vortexing, respectively. X–ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were carried out on a Siemens D500 diffractometer (Siemens AG, Karlsruhe, Germany) operating at a voltage of 35 kV and at a scan rate of 1° min–1 in the scan range of 6–70°° A Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrophotometer (Bruker, Billerica, USA) was used to record the spectra in the range of 400–4000 cm–1 with a resolution of 4 cm–1. A differential scanning calorimeter (DSC) (Mettler Toledo, Switzerland) with a mechanical cooler system was used to analyze the thermal properties of the sorbent. The sorbent was heated and cooled from 0 to 100 °C at a constant rate of 10 °C min–1 to study its melting and crystallization points. The surface morphology of the sorbent was investigated by scanning electron microscope (SEM) using a Mira 3 microscope (Tescan, Czech Republic) with an accelerating voltage of 10.0 kV. The surface area of the sorbent and its average pore diameter were investigated by the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) method using a BELSORP–mini II (BEL, Japan) analyzer using N2 adsorption/desorption at 77 K.

In this work, the Sono–Fenton method was used for the synthesis of GQDs from graphene oxide (GO) [23]. First, GO was prepared from graphite powder according to Hummers method [24]. The prepared GO was finely dispersed into 100 mL of deionized water by ultrasonication for 2 h. Then 30 mL of hydrogen peroxide (30%, w/w) was added under stirring followed by a drop wise addition of aqueous iron (III) chloride solution (2 mg mL–1). Afterward, the reaction mixture was sonicated for about 6 h and dialyzed in deionized water for 3 days in order to complete removal of iron (III) chloride. After dialysis, the solution was freeze–dried to obtain GQDs as a solid material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of poly (ε–caprolactone) grafted graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Poly (ε–caprolactone) grafted graphene quantum dots (PCL–g–GQDs) was synthesized in this study via ring opening polymerization of CL catalyzed by Sn(Oct)2 [25]. At first, 15 mg of the prepared GQDs was dispersed into 3 mL of dry toluene by ultrasonic treatment for 1 h. Then 0.3 mL of CL was added to the mixture under a nitrogen flow followed by drop wisely addition of 100 µL Sn(Oct)2. The mixture placed in an oil bath adjusted at 90 °C under constant stirring. After 3 h the polymerization was stopped by rapid cooling to room temperature. The product was purified by precipitating into large excess of cold n–hexane three times and dried under vacuum. Finally, the collected solid material was dissolved in tetrahydrofuran and centrifuged to remove the PCL homopolymer. This process was repeated twice again and the residue was dried under vacuum.

A 10 mL deionized water spiked with 250 μg L−1 (each analyte) or diluted sample solution (see Section 2.2) was transferred into a 15–mL glass test tube and 0.25 g (2.5%, w/v) NaCl was dissolved in it. Then, 100 mg of the sorbent was added to the solution and the mixture was vortexed for 3 min. After that, the solution was centrifuged for 4 min at 4000 rpm and the analytes–loaded sorbent was settled at the bottom of tube. In the next step, the supernatant solution was removed and the sorbent was eluted with 1.5 mL methanol under sonication for 2 min to desorb the analytes. Afterward, the eluent was removed and mixed with 45 µL 1,1,2–TCE. The mixture was rapidly dispersed into 5 mL deionized water and the resulted cloudy solution was centrifuged for 4 min at 4000 rpm to settle down the 1,1,2–TCE droplets. Finally, 1 µL of the sedimented phase (10 ± 0.5 µL) was taken and injected into the separation system for analysis.

EF is considered as the analyte concentration ratio in the sedimented phase (C sed) to the initial concentration (C 0) in the sample.
(1)
E F =
C sed
C 0

ER refers to the percentage of the total amount of analyte (n 0) which is transferred into the sedimented phase (n sed).
(2)
E R % =
n sed
n 0
× 100 =
C sed
×
V sed
C 0
×
V aq
× 100 = E F ×
V sed
V aq
× 100
where V sed and V aq are the volumes of the sedimented organic phase and aqueous phase, respectively.

In this study, PCL–g–GQDs was used as the sorbent for extraction of the analytes from fruit juice samples. PCL as a hydrophobic polyester is biodegradable, biocompatible, and nontoxic to living organisms. Also, PCL has good resistant ability to water and organic solvent. These unique properties render PCL as an interesting sorbent. GQDs were added to the PCL as nanofiller in order to prevent the aggregation of the polymer particles in the aqueous phase and thus increases the effective surface area. The characteristics of the PCL–g–GQDs were investigated by FTIR, XRD, DSC, SEM, and BET method.

The typical FTIR spectrum of PCL–g–GQDs (Fig. 1 a) reveals bands at 1729, 3438, and 1048 cm–1 which are attributed to the stretching vibrations of >CO, OH, and CO groups, respectively. Furthermore, the peaks at 1294, 1241, and 1181 cm–1 are related to the stretching vibrations of ester group. In addition, the bands at 2945 and 2866 cm–1 attributed to the aliphatic CH stretching vibrations related to the characteristic peaks for the pendant PCL chains. Also, the spectrum indicates the presence of GQDs signature peak around 1600 cm–1 which related to stretching vibration of CC.

To characterize the crystalline structure of the PCL–g–GQDs sorbent, XRD pattern was studied. According to the results (Fig. 1b), the sorbent manifests a typical orthorhombic crystalline unit cell with peaks at Bragg angle 2θ =21.72, 22.24, and 24.04° that can be attributed to 110, 111, and 200 crystallographic planes of PCL, respectively.

The surface morphology and particle size of the sorbent were studied using SEM. The SEM image shown in Fig. 1d, indicates that the sorbent particles exhibits approximately spherical morphology with diameter distribution in the range of 6–10 nm. These results indicated that the sorbent can be considered as a quantum dot.

To investigate the surface area and pore structure of the sorbent, N2 adsorption/desorption experiments were carried out at 77 K. According to the results, the BET surface area and average pore diameter of the sorbent particles are 21 m2 g–1 and 5.8 nm, respectively.

It should be mentioned that since the sorbent was used in the adsorption of the pesticides which has not any relationship with the optical and electronic properties of the sorbent, these properties were not exploited in this study. But, these properties were comprehensively investigated in the previously published paper [25] which the sorbent was synthesized according to it. The results suggested that the sorbent is a quantum dot.

The weight of sorbent is a main factor which can influence the ERs of analytes in DSPE–based methods since it can influence the amount of the analytes adsorbed. To optimize this parameter, various weights of the sorbent (50, 75, 100, 125, and 150 mg) were investigated. Considering the results (Fig. 1S), as the weight of the sorbent increases till 100 mg, the ERs enhance and after that remain constant (p values >0.05). It can be attributed to the fact that as the weight of the adsorbent increases, the contact area between the sorbent and analytes enhances and the probability of adsorbing the analytes is increased. So, 100 mg was selected as the optimal weight of the sorbent.

In the present work, vortex agitation is used for increase the contact interface between the sorbent and the analytes in the aqueous solution which can shorten the extraction time. The influence of this parameter on the ERs of the analytes was investigated in the range of 1 to 5 min. Regarding to the results, the extraction efficiency enhanced continuously by increasing the vortexing time from 1 to 3 min, and remained constant (p values >0.05) with further increase in vortexing period. So, the vortex time of 3 min was selected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selection of type and volume of elution/disperser solvent
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the elution solvent used in DSPE step takes on the role of a disperser solvent in the following DLLME. Therefore, selection of an appropriate solvent is crucial for the effective elution and efficient dispersion of the preconcentration solvent used in DLLME in order to achieve high extraction efficiency of the analytes. To find the suitable solvent, the capability of methanol, ACN, and isopropanol were studied. Considering the results (Fig. 2 S), among these solvents, methanol shows the better results compared with ACN and isopropanol. So, methanol was chosen as an appropriate elution/disperser solvent for the rest of the experiments.

The methanol volume was also evaluated by varying its volume in the range of 0.5 to 2.5 mL in order to identify the optimum volume for the desorption process. The results (Fig. 3 S) show that the ERs of the analytes are increased up to 1.5 mL and then decrease. This can attribute to the fact that at the volumes less than 1.5 mL, the analytes can not be desorbed completely from the sorbent surface and as a result low ERs are obtained. In addition, at volumes higher than 1.5 mL, the ERs start to decrease because the solubility of the analytes into the aqueous phase in DLLME step increases in the presence of high percentage of methanol. Therefore, 1.5 mL methanol was chosen for the further studies.

Generally, addition of a salt decreases the solubility of analytes in aqueous phase and increases their transference into an organic phase [26]. Furthermore, salt addition can also reduce the solubility of the extractive phase in the aqueous solution. To evaluate the effect of ionic strength on DSPE efficiency, several solutions containing different concentrations of NaCl (0–10%, w/v) were prepared and analyzed. According to the results, the extraction efficiency of the analytes increased up to 2.5% (w/v) NaCl and after that remained constant (p values >0.05). Therefore, 2.5% (w/v) NaCl was selected for the subsequent experiments.

In the present study, the influence of desorption time was evaluated by applying sonication in the range of 0–5.0 min. It was found that 2.0 min was sufficient to achieve the complete desorption of the analytes and after 2 min, no significant change occurred in the extraction efficiencies. So, 2.0 min was used as the optimum desorption time in the DSPE procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selection the type of preconcentration solvent
ce:section.ce:para: The selection of an appropriate preconcentration solvent is of great importance to achieve satisfactory results in this work. The preconcentration solvent should meet several criteria including being miscible with the elution solvent and immiscible with the aqueous solution, higher density compared to water, low solubility in water, high extraction capability of the analytes, and good chromatographic behavior. Based on these characteristics four organic solvents including 1,1,1–TCE, 1,2–DBE, chloroform, and 1,1,2–TCE were investigated as the preconcentration solvent. It should be noted that to achieve a similar volume of the sedimented phase (10 ± 0.5 µL) after performing the method, the volumes of 38, 55, 40, and 45 µL of 1,2–DBE, chloroform, 1,1,1–TCE, and 1,1,2–TCE were selected, respectively. The results (Fig. 4S) reveal that 1,1,2–TCE provides the highest extraction efficiency for the analytes. So, it was selected for the further experiments.

The effect of ionic strength in DLLME step was investigated by adding various concentrations of NaCl (0–10%, w/v) into the aqueous solution. On the basis of the results, the ERs of analytes declined with enhancing NaCl concentration. This decline can be attributed to enhance in the viscosity of solution by increasing the salt concentration. Thus, the subsequent experiments were carried out in the absence of salt.

The volume of the preconcentration solvent is another crucial parameter that can directly affects the ERs, EFs, and LODs of the method. Also, it can affect the volume of the sedimented phase. To evaluate this parameter, various volumes of 1,1,2–TCE (45 to 70 μL with the intervals of 5–μL) were tested in the DLLME step. Considering the results, the ERs remained relatively constant (p values > 0.05) by increasing the volume of 1,1,2–TCE from 45 to 70 µL, while the EFs decreased. This phenomenon can be attributed to the dilution effect resulted from enhancement of the sedimented phase volume from 10 to 23 µL. It is noted that using the volumes ≤40 µL, no sedimented phase was formed. Thus, to obtain high EFs and low LODs, 45 μL was selected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of centrifugation speed and time
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, after formation of the cloudy solution in DLLME step, centrifugation was used to isolate the preconcentration solvent containing the analytes from the aqueous solution. To optimize centrifuging speed and time, various experiments were carried out in the ranges of 2000–6000 rpm and 2–6 min, respectively. The results revealed that the ERs slightly increased till 4000 rpm and 4 min and then remained constant at higher values. Therefore, 4000 rpm and 4 min were selected as the centrifugation rate and time, respectively.

Quantitative aspects of the method including LOD (based on signal to noise ratio of 3), limit of quantification (LOQ, based on signal to noise ratio of 10), linear range (LR), determination coefficient (r 2), relative standard deviation (RSD), EF, and ER were investigated under the optimal conditions. The results are summarized in Table 1 . The LODs and LOQs were within the ranges of 0.32–0.76 and 1.1–2.6 ng mL–1, respectively. Broad LRs with excellent linearities (r 2 ≥ 0.9942) were obtained for the analytes. The precision of the method, expressed as RSD%, was studied by analyzing standard solutions at a concentration of 10 ng mL−1 of each analyte on the same day (n =6) and four different days. The RSDs% were in the ranges of 4.6–6.7 and 5.2–8.4% for intra– and inter–day precisions, respectively. The ERs ranged from 68 to 84%. The EFs ranged from 680 to 840 for deionized water and 340 to 420 for fruit juices. The results indicate that the procedure is suitable for analyzing the studied pesticides at trace levels. It should be mentioned that in order to demonstrate the usefulness of the combined mode (DSPE–DLLME), some experiments were also performed using individual DSPE and DLLME. According to the results by using alone DSPE, the obtained LODs were higher than 63 ng mL–1 which were higher than those of the combined mode and indicated that this mode is not suitable for analysis of the pesticide residues in juice samples. In the case of DLLME the obtained EFs (760–910) and LODs (0.21–0.58 ng mL–1) were better than the combined mode for six analytes but a large peak was appeared in the chromatograms of apricot, apple, watermelon, and pomegranate juices which overlap those of oxadiazon, diniconazole, and clodinafop–propargyl and made their analysis impossible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application for analysis of real samples
ce:section.ce:para: After optimization and validation studies, the applicability of the method was evaluated for the extraction and determination of some pesticides in various fruit juices including apple, pomegranate, grape, orange, watermelon, sour cherry, and apricot. Fig. 2 indicates GC–FID chromatograms of directly injected standard solution (50 mg L–1 of each analyte in 1,1,2–TCE), spiked grape juice (25 ng mL–1 of each analyte), and unspiked grape juice after applying the method. Comparison of the chromatograms of standard solution and the grape juice sample indicate that a peak is observed at the retention time belongs to penconazole. To more identify the compound emerged in the retention time of penconazole in the chromatogram of GC–FID, the grape juice was also analyzed by GC–MS after performing the proposed extraction procedure. The GC–total ions current (TIC)–MS chromatogram obtained for the grape juice along with the mass spectrum of penconazole and scan 948 (retention time 11.286) shown in Fig. 3, verify the existence of penconazole in this sample. The penconazole concentration in the grape juice was calculated 24 ± 1 ng mL–1 (n =3) from GC–FID data. Other samples were free of the analytes. To evaluate matrix effect of the samples on the efficiency of the approach, the added–found method was used. For this purpose, the fruit juice samples and deionized water were separately spiked with the analytes at four different concentrations (3, 10, 25, and 50 ng mL–1 of each analyte) and the results for the samples in comparison with deionized water are expressed as mean relative recoveries and listed in Table 2 . Mean relative recoveries from 84 to 104% for fruit juices reveal that the matrices of these samples have no significant effect on the performing of the method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the method with other approaches
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical characteristics (RSD, LOD, EF, ER, and LR) of the method and other previously published approaches for the analysis of the target analytes in different samples are compared and summarized in Table 3 . The repeatability of the method is good and the RSDs are comparable or better than the others. The LODs of the method are comparable or better than those of the other mentioned methods, except in the cases in which MS has been used as the detection system that is inherently more sensitive than FID. As it is seen from the results, in comparison with other approaches, this method shows very high EFs. The ERs of the method are higher than or comparable with those of other mentioned methods. Also, this method has comparable or wider LRs compared to the other mentioned methods.

In the present work, a sample pretreatment procedure based on a combination of DSPE and DLLME was introduced as an efficient method for the extraction of some pesticides from fruit juice samples before their determination by GC–FID. In this research, for the first time, PCL–g–GQDs was exploited as an efficient sorbent in DSPE step. As the outcomes indicate, the method has some remarkable characteristics such as high ERs (68–84%) and EFs (680–840), low LODs (0.32–0.76 ng mL–1) and LOQs (1.1–2.6 ng mL–1), and an acceptable repeatability (RSD ≤ 8.4%). These characteristics reveal that the established analytical approach can be reliably used for the determination of trace levels of pesticides (ng mL–1) in them.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: Ali Mohebbi declares that he has no conflict of interest. Mir Ali Farajzadeh declares that he has no conflict of interest. Ahad Mahmoudzadeh declares that he has no conflict of interest. Asma Etemady declares that she has no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the Research Council of University of Tabrizfor financial support.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.microc.2019.104328.
